clinicalStem: A 13-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department at 2 AM by his parents with sudden-onset severe right scrotal pain that woke him from sleep 3 hours ago. He has vomited twice. On examination, the right testis is high-riding and tender, with a transverse lie. There is no cremasteric reflex on the right side. The left testis is normal. Doppler ultrasound is not immediately available. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Immediate surgical exploration without waiting for imaging
Arrange urgent Doppler ultrasound before any intervention
Admit for observation and analgesia, reassess in 6 hours
Start IV antibiotics for epididymo-orchitis and monitor
Attempt manual detorsion and schedule elective orchidopexy
explanation: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency where the viability of the testis is time-critical: salvage rates approach 100% if operated within 6 hours but fall to <10% after 24 hours. The classic triad of sudden-onset severe pain, high-riding testis with transverse lie, and absent cremasteric reflex is highly specific. When clinical suspicion is high, imaging must NOT delay surgical exploration.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. With a clinical picture this classic — sudden nocturnal onset, high-riding transverse testis, absent cremasteric reflex, vomiting — immediate surgical exploration is mandated. Every hour of delay worsens testicular salvage rates.
Incorrect. Doppler ultrasound should not delay theatre when clinical suspicion is high. It is useful in equivocal cases; this presentation is unequivocal.
Incorrect. Observation is dangerous. After 6 hours salvage rates drop significantly; this patient is already 3 hours in.
Incorrect. The absent cremasteric reflex, high-riding testis, and acute onset do not support epididymo-orchitis. Antibiotics without surgery will lead to testicular loss.
Incorrect. Manual detorsion may be attempted as a temporising measure if theatre is unavailable, but it does not replace surgical orchidopexy, and this option describes only elective follow-up.
clinicalPearl: Absent cremasteric reflex + high-riding transverse testis = testicular torsion until proven otherwise — take to theatre immediately, do not wait for ultrasound.
keyClue: Absent cremasteric reflex with high-riding transverse testis after sudden nocturnal onset
commonExamTrap: Ordering Doppler ultrasound before surgery when the clinical diagnosis is already clear — imaging delays cost the testis.
missedConcept: Time-to-detorsion is the key determinant of testicular salvage; clinical diagnosis alone is sufficient to proceed to theatre.
redFlagReason: Testicular torsion is a urological emergency; delay beyond 6 hours causes irreversible ischaemia and loss of the testis.

clinicalStem: A 9-year-old boy presents with a 2-day history of gradually worsening left scrotal pain. His mother says the pain began mildly and has never woken him from sleep. On examination, he is afebrile, the left cremasteric reflex is intact, and there is a small tender nodule at the upper pole of the left testis. A blue-black discolouration is visible through the overlying scrotal skin. The testis itself is non-tender. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Torsion of the appendix testis
Testicular torsion
Epididymo-orchitis
Henoch-Schönlein purpura affecting the scrotum
Incarcerated inguinal hernia
explanation: Torsion of the appendix testis (hydatid of Morgagni) classically presents in pre-pubertal boys (7–12 years) with gradual-onset pain localised to the upper pole. The pathognomonic 'blue dot sign' — a blue-black spot visible through the scrotal skin — represents the infarcted appendage. The cremasteric reflex is preserved and the testis itself is non-tender, distinguishing it from true testicular torsion.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Gradual onset, upper-pole nodule tenderness, intact cremasteric reflex, non-tender testis, and the blue dot sign all point to torsion of the appendix testis.
Incorrect. Testicular torsion presents with sudden onset, a high-riding testis with transverse lie, absent cremasteric reflex, and global testicular tenderness — none of which are present here.

Incorrect. Epididymo-orchitis typically occurs in sexually active adolescents or infants with UTI, presents with fever, dysuria, and diffuse epididymal/testicular tenderness, and lacks the blue dot sign.
Incorrect. HSP can cause scrotal oedema and pain, but it is associated with the characteristic purpuric rash on the lower limbs and buttocks, arthralgia, and abdominal pain.
Incorrect. An incarcerated hernia presents with a groin swelling that may extend into the scrotum, bowel obstruction symptoms, and is not associated with an upper-pole nodule or the blue dot sign.
clinicalPearl: The 'blue dot sign' at the upper pole of the testis in a pre-pubertal boy with gradual-onset pain and an intact cremasteric reflex is pathognomonic of torsion of the appendix testis.
keyClue: Blue-black discolouration at upper pole of testis with intact cremasteric reflex and non-tender testis
commonExamTrap: Confusing upper-pole tenderness with testicular torsion — in appendix torsion, point tenderness is at the upper pole and the testis itself is non-tender.
missedConcept: Appendix testis torsion can be managed conservatively with analgesia; the distinction from true testicular torsion is clinically critical.

clinicalStem: A 5-year-old boy is brought to clinic with a 3-day history of right scrotal swelling and pain. His temperature is 38.4°C. He has been having urinary frequency and dysuria for the past week. On examination, the right epididymis is markedly tender and swollen; the testis is normally positioned with a positive cremasteric reflex. Urine dipstick shows leucocytes and nitrites. What is the most important investigation to arrange after this episode has resolved?
options: Renal and bladder ultrasound with voiding cystourethrogram
Testicular Doppler ultrasound
Urine culture and sensitivity only
Scrotal MRI to assess testicular viability
Serum testosterone and gonadotrophins
explanation: Epididymo-orchitis in a pre-pubertal boy is uncommon and is frequently associated with an underlying urological anomaly such as vesico-ureteric reflux, posterior urethral valves, or ureterocele. A renal-bladder ultrasound and voiding cystourethrogram (MCUG/VCUG) should be arranged after the acute episode resolves to identify any structural abnormality predisposing to recurrent UTI and ascending infection.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Pre-pubertal epididymo-orchitis is strongly associated with underlying urological anomalies. Imaging of the urinary tract to detect reflux, obstruction, or anatomical variants is mandatory after the acute episode.
Incorrect. Testicular Doppler is used acutely to distinguish torsion from orchitis; it does not investigate the underlying cause of epididymo-orchitis in a child.
Incorrect. Urine culture is essential during the acute phase but alone does not investigate structural causes of recurrent infection.
Incorrect. Scrotal MRI is not routinely indicated and does not help identify the urological predisposing factors in this age group.
Incorrect. Hormonal assessment is not relevant here; this is an infectious, not an endocrine, problem.
clinicalPearl: Epididymo-orchitis in a pre-pubertal boy should prompt investigation for underlying urological anomaly — arrange renal ultrasound and MCUG/VCUG after the acute episode.
keyClue: 5-year-old pre-pubertal boy with epididymo-orchitis and concurrent UTI symptoms
commonExamTrap: Treating the infection and discharging without investigating for structural urological abnormality in a young child.
missedConcept: In pre-pubertal boys, epididymo-orchitis is unusual and warrants urological workup; in adolescents sexually transmitted organisms should also be considered.

subtopic: Distinguishing from other causes of acute scrotum
clinicalStem: A 6-year-old boy is brought by his father with a 1-day history of painless redness and swelling of the scrotum. There is no history of trauma. The swelling is soft, non-tender, and bright red, extending onto the perineum and inguinal region bilaterally. The testes are easily palpable and non-tender. He is afebrile. He is eating and drinking normally. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Idiopathic scrotal oedema
Testicular torsion
Acute epididymo-orchitis
Incarcerated inguinal hernia
Henoch-Schönlein purpura
explanation: Idiopathic scrotal oedema is a self-limiting condition most common in boys aged 5–11 years. It presents with painless, erythematous, and oedematous swelling of the scrotum and surrounding perineum, with normal, non-tender, easily palpable testes. It is benign and resolves spontaneously within 2–5 days. The bilateral extension to perineum and inguinal region, absence of pain, and non-tender testes are characteristic.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Painless, bright-red, bilateral scrotal oedema extending to the perineum with normal non-tender palpable testes in an afebrile child is classic idiopathic scrotal oedema.
Incorrect. Testicular torsion causes severe unilateral testicular pain, a high-riding tender testis, and absent cremasteric reflex — none of which are present here.
Incorrect. Epididymo-orchitis causes tender epididymal/testicular swelling, typically with fever and urinary symptoms.
Incorrect. An incarcerated hernia presents with a painful irreducible groin or scrotal mass and signs of bowel obstruction.
Incorrect. HSP scrotal involvement is part of a systemic illness with purpuric rash on the lower limbs/buttocks, abdominal pain, and arthralgia.
clinicalPearl: Painless bright-red scrotal oedema extending to the perineum with normal, non-tender testes in a pre-pubertal boy = idiopathic scrotal oedema; reassure and observe.
keyClue: Painless bright-red swelling extending to perineum with normal non-tender testes bilaterally
commonExamTrap: Mistaking the erythema for infection or torsion — the key is that the testes themselves are non-tender and easily palpable.
missedConcept: Idiopathic scrotal oedema is benign and self-resolving; correctly identifying it avoids unnecessary surgical exploration.

clinicalStem: A 14-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department 4 hours after taking approximately 30 tablets of paracetamol 500 mg (15 g total) in a deliberate self-harm attempt. She is conscious, alert, and currently asymptomatic with no nausea or vomiting. Vital signs are normal. Liver function tests are currently normal. A serum paracetamol level is sent. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Administer IV N-acetylcysteine (NAC) immediately while awaiting paracetamol level
Observe and repeat liver function tests in 24 hours before deciding on treatment
Perform gastric lavage and activated charcoal, then discharge once the level returns normal
Administer oral methionine as the preferred antidote in this adolescent
Wait for the 4-hour paracetamol level to plot on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram before any treatment
explanation: When a patient presents with a clearly toxic paracetamol ingestion (>150 mg/kg or >7.5 g in a teenager) and is within the treatment window, N-acetylcysteine should be started immediately — especially when the level is pending and the dose history is reliable. At 15 g in a 14-year-old, the ingestion is almost certainly above the treatment line. NAC is the definitive antidote that replenishes glutathione stores and prevents hepatotoxicity. Early treatment is essential; delaying for the level risks missing the therapeutic window.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. A 15 g ingestion in an adolescent is well above the toxic threshold. NAC should be commenced immediately — the dose is clearly toxic, and waiting risks hepatocellular damage.
Incorrect. With a clearly toxic ingestion and a 4-hour window, watchful waiting without NAC is dangerous and allows ongoing hepatotoxicity to develop.

Incorrect. Activated charcoal may be useful within 1 hour of ingestion; at 4 hours its benefit is minimal. Discharging based only on a normal initial level is incorrect — hepatotoxicity typically develops 24–72 hours later.
Incorrect. Oral methionine is an alternative antidote used in some settings but IV NAC is the gold-standard first-line treatment, particularly in a hospital setting with a large ingestion.
Incorrect. The 4-hour level and nomogram are useful when the ingestion dose is uncertain; here the dose is clearly toxic, making immediate NAC the correct action without waiting.
clinicalPearl: In paracetamol overdose, start NAC immediately if the ingestion is clearly above the toxic threshold — do not wait for a level when the dose is already known to be dangerous.
keyClue: 15 g paracetamol ingestion in an adolescent — 4 hours post-ingestion, currently asymptomatic
commonExamTrap: Being reassured by current absence of symptoms and normal LFTs — paracetamol hepatotoxicity is delayed 24–72 hours and is preventable only with prompt NAC.
missedConcept: Paracetamol toxicity causes delayed hepatocellular necrosis; early NAC prevents this even when the patient appears well at presentation.
redFlagReason: Paracetamol overdose causes progressive hepatotoxicity and fulminant liver failure if NAC is not administered promptly; delay in treatment is potentially fatal.

clinicalStem: A 7-year-old boy from a farming community is brought to the emergency department unconscious. His grandfather reports he was playing near pesticide canisters an hour ago. On examination: HR 48 bpm, RR 8 breaths/min with audible wheeze, pupils bilaterally pinpoint (2 mm), profuse salivation, lacrimation, urinary incontinence, and muscle fasciculations. GCS is 8/15. SpO2 is 82% on room air. Which drug is the single most important first-line antidote?
options: Atropine IV
Pralidoxime IV
Diazepam IV
Naloxone IV
Activated charcoal via nasogastric tube
explanation: This child has a classic cholinergic toxidrome from organophosphate poisoning: bradycardia, bronchospasm, miosis, SLUDGE/DUMBELS signs (salivation, lacrimation, urination, defaecation/bronchospasm, emesis, lacrimation, secretions). Atropine is the primary antidote — it competitively blocks muscarinic receptors, reversing bronchospasm, excessive secretions, and bradycardia. High-dose atropine (titrated to secretion drying) is life-saving. Pralidoxime reactivates inhibited acetylcholinesterase but is secondary to atropine and less effective if delayed.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Atropine is the cornerstone antidote for organophosphate poisoning. It reverses the life-threatening bronchospasm and secretions — the main cause of death — by blocking muscarinic acetylcholine receptors. Large, repeated doses are often needed.

Incorrect. Pralidoxime is an adjunct that can reactivate acetylcholinesterase if given early, but it is secondary to atropine. It does not reverse bronchospasm as rapidly and is ineffective once ageing of the enzyme has occurred.
Incorrect. Diazepam may be used for organophosphate-induced seizures, but it does not address the cholinergic crisis and is not the primary antidote.
Incorrect. Naloxone reverses opioid toxidrome (miosis + bradypnoea + reduced consciousness); however, the SLUDGE signs here are not features of opioid poisoning.
Incorrect. Activated charcoal may reduce further absorption but is absolutely contraindicated in an unconscious patient at risk of aspiration, and does not reverse the toxidrome.
clinicalPearl: In organophosphate poisoning, give atropine IV in large doses, titrated to drying of secretions — not to heart rate. Bronchospasm and secretions kill; pralidoxime is adjunctive.
keyClue: Pinpoint pupils + SLUDGE signs + bradycardia + bronchospasm after pesticide exposure
commonExamTrap: Choosing pralidoxime as the first-line antidote — atropine must come first and takes priority in the acute resuscitation.
missedConcept: Atropine is titrated to secretion drying (not HR); the goal endpoint is dry lungs, not a target heart rate.
redFlagReason: Organophosphate poisoning causes life-threatening bronchospasm and respiratory failure; without immediate atropine the child will die from hypoxia.

clinicalStem: A 3-year-old girl is brought to A&E 2 hours after ingesting an unknown number of her mother's iron tablets. She initially had vomiting and bloody diarrhoea (phase 1). She now appears calm, with no vomiting, and her parents are relieved. Vital signs: HR 105 bpm, BP 85/50 mmHg, RR 22/min. Abdominal X-ray shows multiple radio-opaque tablets in the stomach. Serum iron is 480 μmol/L (normal <30 μmol/L). What is the correct interpretation of her current clinical state?
options: She is in the deceptive phase of iron toxicity — she remains at high risk and requires immediate deferoxamine treatment
She has recovered; discharge with advice to parents
She has mild iron toxicity and can be managed with activated charcoal alone
The calm phase indicates the worst is over; IV fluids alone are sufficient
She requires emergency surgery to remove the tablet fragments
explanation: Iron poisoning classically progresses through five phases. Phase 2 is the 'latent' or deceptive phase (2–12 hours post-ingestion) where symptoms temporarily improve, creating a false sense of recovery. However, a serum iron of 480 μmol/L far exceeds the toxic threshold (~55 μmol/L) and the visible tablets on X-ray indicate ongoing absorption. Deferoxamine, the chelating antidote, is urgently indicated. Discharging during this phase is a potentially fatal error.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. The apparent calm is the deceptive phase — not recovery. Markedly elevated serum iron confirms severe toxicity. Deferoxamine must be commenced immediately to chelate free iron and prevent cardiovascular collapse and hepatotoxicity in phase 3.
Incorrect. Discharging in the latent phase of iron toxicity is dangerous and could be fatal. The serum iron level indicates severe poisoning.
Incorrect. Activated charcoal does not bind iron and is not effective in iron overdose.
Incorrect. IV fluids treat hypovolaemia but do not remove iron. The serum iron level mandates chelation therapy with deferoxamine.
Incorrect. Whole-bowel irrigation with polyethylene glycol may help clear iron tablets from the gut, but surgery is not the treatment; deferoxamine chelation is.
clinicalPearl: The apparent calm in iron poisoning (phase 2) is deceptive — a high serum iron confirms ongoing toxicity and mandates deferoxamine chelation, not discharge.
keyClue: Apparent improvement after initial GI symptoms + markedly elevated serum iron + radio-opaque tablets visible
commonExamTrap: Interpreting the latent phase as recovery and discharging the child — this is one of the classic fatal errors in toxicology management.
missedConcept: Iron poisoning has a biphasic course; the deceptive latent phase does not mean the patient is safe — serum iron level guides treatment decision.
redFlagReason: Severe iron toxicity can progress from the latent phase to cardiovascular collapse, metabolic acidosis, and multi-organ failure within hours if chelation is not started.

clinicalStem: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after his mother found him unresponsive 20 minutes ago. She had methadone prescribed for opioid dependence in an unlocked bedside drawer. On examination: GCS 6/15, RR 6 breaths/min, SpO2 74% on room air, bilateral pinpoint pupils, he is floppy with absent gag reflex. What is the single most important immediate intervention?
options: Administer IV/IM naloxone and prepare for airway management
Insert an oropharyngeal airway and commence bag-mask ventilation alone
Perform activated charcoal via nasogastric tube urgently
Arrange an urgent CT head to exclude intracranial haemorrhage
Administer IV glucose to treat hypoglycaemia as the most likely cause
explanation: This child has a classic opioid toxidrome: miosis, respiratory depression, reduced consciousness, and hypotonia after access to methadone. The immediate priority follows ABCDE — naloxone (opioid antagonist) reverses all features of opioid toxicity including respiratory depression. Airway management with bag-mask ventilation is concurrent and essential given SpO2 of 74%, but naloxone must also be given to reverse the cause. Methadone's long half-life means repeated doses or an infusion may be required.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Naloxone reverses opioid-induced respiratory depression, miosis, and coma. Combined with airway support, it is the correct simultaneous response. In methadone poisoning, repeated dosing or continuous infusion is often needed due to methadone's long half-life.
Incorrect. Bag-mask ventilation addresses hypoxia but does not reverse the opioid toxidrome. Naloxone must be co-administered to restore respiratory drive.
Incorrect. Activated charcoal is contraindicated in an unconscious patient due to aspiration risk and does not reverse acute opioid toxicity.
Incorrect. CT head is not the priority here — the clinical picture is fully explained by opioid poisoning and airway management takes precedence.

Incorrect. While hypoglycaemia should be checked (and treated if present), the classic opioid toxidrome here — especially the pinpoint pupils and context of methadone access — makes opioid poisoning the unequivocal diagnosis.
clinicalPearl: Miosis + respiratory depression + reduced GCS in a toddler with opioid access = naloxone immediately; methadone's long half-life often necessitates repeated naloxone doses or infusion.
keyClue: Pinpoint pupils + RR 6 + access to methadone in a 2-year-old
commonExamTrap: Providing only supportive care without giving naloxone — the antidote reverses the cause and can be life-saving.
missedConcept: Methadone has a prolonged half-life (24–36 hours); a single dose of naloxone may not be sufficient and recurrence of toxicity is common.
redFlagReason: SpO2 of 74% with RR 6 represents imminent respiratory arrest; without immediate naloxone and airway support the child will die.

topic: Acute severe asthma in a child
clinicalStem: A 9-year-old boy with known asthma arrives in the emergency department. His mother says he has been wheezing for 6 hours and has used his salbutamol inhaler 8 times today with minimal relief. On examination: RR 36/min, HR 130 bpm, SpO2 90% on room air, he can speak in words only. There is marked accessory muscle use and severe intercostal recession. Peak flow is 38% of predicted. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: High-flow oxygen, back-to-back salbutamol nebulisations, and IV/IM systemic corticosteroids
Continue salbutamol MDI via spacer and add an inhaled corticosteroid
Commence IV aminophylline infusion as first-line treatment
Arrange chest X-ray and blood gas before starting treatment
Perform immediate rapid-sequence intubation and mechanical ventilation
explanation: This child has acute severe asthma (BTS/SIGN criteria: SpO2 <92%, PEFR 33–50% predicted, unable to complete sentences, HR >125 bpm). Initial management is high-flow oxygen (target SpO2 ≥94%), back-to-back (every 20–30 minutes) salbutamol nebulisations driven by oxygen, and systemic corticosteroids (oral prednisolone or IV hydrocortisone). Ipratropium bromide should be added. This is the established first-line approach before escalating to IV magnesium or aminophylline.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Acute severe asthma requires immediate high-flow O2, frequent nebulised salbutamol (with ipratropium), and systemic steroids to reduce airway inflammation. This is the BTS/SIGN recommended first-line approach.
Incorrect. MDI via spacer delivers inadequate drug in acute severe asthma; nebulisations driven by oxygen are required. Adding an ICS alone does not address the acute exacerbation adequately.
Incorrect. IV aminophylline is a second- or third-line agent used when first-line treatments (O2, nebulisers, steroids, magnesium) have failed. It has a narrow therapeutic index.
Incorrect. Treatment must not be delayed for investigations. Oxygen and bronchodilators should be started immediately; investigations can follow.
Incorrect. Intubation is a last resort reserved for respiratory failure (exhaustion, rising PaCO2, decreasing consciousness). This child is sick but not at that point yet.
clinicalPearl: Acute severe asthma: start O2 + back-to-back salbutamol nebulisations + systemic steroids immediately — a normal or rising CO2 in acute asthma signals impending respiratory failure.
keyClue: PEFR 38% + SpO2 90% + speaking in words only + accessory muscle use
commonExamTrap: Escalating directly to intubation or aminophylline without first giving optimised nebulisations, oxygen, and steroids.
missedConcept: A normal PaCO2 in a severely breathless asthmatic child is paradoxically dangerous — it suggests the child is tiring and approaching respiratory failure.
redFlagReason: Acute severe asthma with SpO2 90% and PEFR 38% can progress rapidly to life-threatening asthma and respiratory arrest without immediate treatment.

clinicalStem: A 7-year-old girl is referred by her GP with a 12-month history of nocturnal cough (3–4 nights per week), intermittent wheeze triggered by exercise and viral infections, and two episodes of shortness of breath in the past year. Her symptoms improve with her older brother's salbutamol inhaler. There is a family history of eczema and hay fever. Spirometry shows FEV1/FVC of 0.69 (predicted >0.80). Which finding would most strongly confirm the diagnosis of asthma?
options: ≥12% and ≥200 mL improvement in FEV1 after bronchodilator administration
A positive skin prick test to house dust mite
Peripheral blood eosinophilia >400 cells/μL
A chest X-ray showing hyperinflation
A positive methacholine challenge test showing PC20 of 16 mg/mL
explanation: The diagnostic hallmark of asthma is reversible airflow obstruction. In spirometry, a post-bronchodilator improvement of ≥12% AND ≥200 mL in FEV1 (bronchodilator reversibility test) confirms the diagnosis. This child already has obstructive spirometry (FEV1/FVC below predicted) consistent with asthma. Atopic markers (skin prick test, eosinophilia) support the allergic phenotype but do not confirm the diagnosis. Methacholine PC20 of 16 mg/mL is actually a negative challenge (positive is <8 mg/mL).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Bronchodilator reversibility (≥12% + ≥200 mL increase in FEV1) is the objective diagnostic criterion for asthma when obstructive spirometry is already demonstrated.
Incorrect. A positive skin prick test confirms atopy, which is associated with asthma, but many atopic children do not have asthma and vice versa. It does not confirm the diagnosis.
Incorrect. Eosinophilia supports an eosinophilic/atopic inflammatory phenotype but is non-specific for asthma and can occur in other conditions.
Incorrect. Hyperinflation on CXR may be seen during an acute attack or in severe disease, but CXR is primarily used to exclude other diagnoses and is not diagnostic of asthma.
Incorrect. A methacholine PC20 of 16 mg/mL is a negative result (no significant bronchial hyperresponsiveness); a positive test requires PC20 <8 mg/mL in children.
clinicalPearl: Asthma diagnosis rests on demonstrating reversible airflow obstruction — a ≥12% AND ≥200 mL improvement in FEV1 after salbutamol confirms bronchodilator reversibility.
keyClue: Obstructive spirometry (FEV1/FVC 0.69) + classic symptom pattern in an atopic child
commonExamTrap: Accepting eosinophilia or atopy alone as diagnostic — these support but do not confirm asthma; reversibility on spirometry is the key.
missedConcept: Both the percentage AND absolute value criteria must be met for bronchodilator reversibility — ≥12% AND ≥200 mL.

topic: Step-up therapy in uncontrolled childhood asthma
clinicalStem: A 10-year-old boy has been diagnosed with asthma for 2 years. He currently uses a salbutamol MDI as needed and a low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (beclomethasone 200 μg/day). Despite this regimen, his mother reports he has daytime symptoms 3–4 days per week, wakes with coughing 2–3 nights per week, and his activity is somewhat limited. He requires his reliever inhaler on most days. His inhaler technique is correct and adherence is confirmed. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Add a long-acting beta-2 agonist (LABA) such as salmeterol to his regimen
Increase the inhaled corticosteroid to high dose as the next step
Replace the ICS with a leukotriene receptor antagonist (LTRA)
Add an oral theophylline preparation
Refer immediately for anti-IgE therapy (omalizumab)
explanation: According to BTS/SIGN stepwise management, for children over 5 years with asthma uncontrolled on low-dose ICS (Step 2), the preferred Step 3 addition is a long-acting beta-2 agonist (LABA). Adding salmeterol or formoterol to the existing ICS is the recommended next step. If there is no response to LABA, it should be stopped and alternatives (LTRA, theophylline, or increased ICS) considered. Anti-IgE (omalizumab) is a step 5 treatment reserved for severe allergic asthma.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Adding a LABA to low-dose ICS is the recommended BTS/SIGN Step 3 intervention for children ≥5 years with inadequately controlled asthma on Step 2 therapy.

Incorrect. Escalating to high-dose ICS is associated with significant systemic side effects (growth suppression, adrenal suppression) and is not preferred before a LABA trial. High-dose ICS is step 4.
Incorrect. Replacing the ICS with an LTRA alone removes proven anti-inflammatory therapy; LTRA can be added as an alternative add-on but should not replace ICS.
Incorrect. Theophylline is a step 4 or adjunct option with a narrow therapeutic index and significant side-effect profile; it is not the preferred step 3 add-on.
Incorrect. Omalizumab is reserved for step 5, severe persistent allergic asthma — this child has not yet been tried on LABA or other step 3 options.
clinicalPearl: In children ≥5 years uncontrolled on low-dose ICS, the BTS/SIGN step 3 recommendation is to add a LABA before escalating to high-dose ICS.
keyClue: Symptoms on most days on low-dose ICS with correct inhaler technique and confirmed adherence
commonExamTrap: Jumping to high-dose ICS instead of adding a LABA — LABA addition is preferred at step 3 due to improved efficacy and a more favourable safety profile.
missedConcept: Stepwise management ensures the minimum effective therapy — always confirm technique and adherence before stepping up.

clinicalStem: A 12-year-old girl with severe asthma is in the resuscitation room. She has received three back-to-back salbutamol nebulisations, IV hydrocortisone, IV magnesium sulphate, and is on 15 L/min oxygen via non-rebreather mask. SpO2 is 87%, RR has slowed from 38 to 24 breaths/min, and she is becoming increasingly drowsy. Her chest is now very quiet with minimal air entry bilaterally. PEFR cannot be measured. Arterial blood gas: pH 7.28, PaCO2 6.9 kPa, PaO2 7.8 kPa. Which single finding is most indicative of imminent respiratory arrest requiring anaesthetic review for intubation?
options: Rising PaCO2 (6.9 kPa) with worsening acidosis despite maximum medical therapy
SpO2 of 87% on high-flow oxygen alone
A respiratory rate that has decreased from 38 to 24 breaths/min
Inability to perform peak flow measurement
Requirement for IV magnesium sulphate
explanation: In acute asthma, hyperventilation normally causes hypocapnia (low PaCO2). A normal or rising PaCO2 in a severely breathless asthmatic child is an ominous sign of respiratory muscle fatigue and impending respiratory failure. Here PaCO2 is 6.9 kPa (elevated), with acidosis (pH 7.28) and a slowing respiratory rate — the child is tiring and losing the compensatory tachypnoea. This is a life-threatening asthma feature mandating immediate anaesthetic review for intubation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Rising PaCO2 in an acute asthmatic is the cardinal sign of respiratory failure — the child is no longer able to maintain the hyperventilatory drive needed to blow off CO2. This signals impending arrest and mandates urgent intubation assessment.

Incorrect. SpO2 of 87% is concerning but can occur in severe asthma without impending arrest; taken alone it directs oxygen therapy. It is the combination with hypercapnia and altered consciousness that signals failure.

Incorrect. A decreasing respiratory rate is alarming and suggests fatigue, but it requires interpretation in context. Alone it may reflect improving obstruction; combined with rising CO2 and drowsiness it confirms deterioration.
Incorrect. Inability to perform PEFR is a feature of severe or life-threatening asthma but does not specifically indicate the degree of ventilatory failure as directly as blood gas interpretation.
Incorrect. IV magnesium is a second-line treatment for acute severe asthma and does not itself indicate impending arrest — it indicates that first-line treatment was insufficient.
clinicalPearl: A rising or normal PaCO2 in a breathless asthmatic child is a red flag for impending respiratory arrest — call anaesthetics immediately for intubation assessment.
keyClue: Rising PaCO2 (6.9 kPa) + pH 7.28 + drowsiness + silent chest despite maximum therapy
commonExamTrap: Interpreting the slowing RR as clinical improvement when it actually reflects respiratory muscle exhaustion.
missedConcept: In asthma, tachypnoea drives hypocapnia — a 'normal' CO2 in this context is abnormal and indicates the patient is tiring dangerously.
redFlagReason: Rising PaCO2 with acidosis and decreasing consciousness in a child with acute asthma indicates impending respiratory arrest; delay in securing the airway is fatal.
